title: Barszcz Czerwony
subtitle: Polish red borscht
excerpt: Clear, jewel-red beetroot broth seasoned with bay, allspice, and a sharp hit of vinegar. Drunk from a cup or served in a bowl with a small uszka (tiny mushroom-filled dumplings) on Christmas Eve.
sourceNotes: Barszcz czerwony (red borscht) is one of the oldest dishes in Polish cooking. The clear version, barszcz czysty, served with uszka is the traditional first course of Wigilia (Christmas Eve dinner). Documented in Maria Lemnis and Henryk Vitry, Old Polish Traditions in the Kitchen and at the Table (1979) and Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The colour brightens on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch and freeze half.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tastes better the next day. Reheat gently — do not boil hard or it loses its colour.

content.text: The colour is everything in barszcz. A good barszcz is a deep, translucent ruby — not brown, not murky, not grey. To keep it vibrant, the soup must never boil hard after the beetroot goes in (heat destroys the pigment), and a splash of vinegar or lemon juice is essential: acid stabilises the colour and sharpens the flavour.

This recipe makes a clear, clean barszcz. For the traditional Christmas Eve version, strain out all the solids after cooking and serve as a consommé-style broth. For a more filling everyday version, leave the vegetables in or blend a portion back in.

items.prepNote: raw, peeled and coarsely grated or roughly chopped
optional, if the beetroot is very sharp

content.text: Put all the vegetables, garlic, bay leaves, and allspice in a large saucepan. Add the stock and bring slowly to a simmer. Cook over low heat for 45 to 60 minutes until the beetroot is completely soft and has given all its colour to the broth. Do not boil hard at any point.

Strain the broth through a fine sieve, pressing the vegetables to extract every drop of colour. Discard the solids. Add the red wine vinegar, salt, pepper, and sugar if using. Taste — the broth should be deeply savoury with a clear acidic edge. Reheat gently without boiling. Serve in cups or bowls with a drizzle of soured cream or with uszka alongside.

items.cause: Boiled hard; heat destroys the betacyanin pigment in beetroot
items.fix: Keep at a gentle simmer throughout; once the colour is gone it cannot be recovered — prevent by controlling the heat
Add more vinegar a teaspoon at a time — the sour-sweet balance is everything; also check the salt level

content.text: Barszcz czerwony is the first course of Wigilia, the Polish Christmas Eve supper — a meatless meal of 12 dishes. The clear barszcz arrives in cups, followed by uszka (tiny dumplings filled with dried mushroom and onion), then pickled herring, carp, and pierogis. Outside the Christmas context, barszcz is daily winter food, poured into a mug from the hob.
